post: Government has one essential job: protect the people. When King George III dissolved colonial governments, he didn't just remove leadership, he left the colonies without any legal protection from invasion or internal instability. Jefferson called it near-anarchy. And for a generation steeped in Enlightenment thinking, that failure had a name: a broken social contract. Locke, Rousseau, and others had articulated the idea clearly: the people agree to be governed, the government agrees to protect them. When the government stops holding up its end, the people are no longer bound by theirs. Grievance 6 is Jefferson making that argument in plain terms. The king had dissolved his own legitimacy. What came next was the right to start over. Dr. Matthew Goetz of White House Historical Association explains in Episode 6 of our 27-part series. Join Constitution Day Live →  https://lnkd.in/eRwWh7DZ  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #SocialContract  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #AmericanRevolution

"To demote politics is to elevate the limitless opportunity of every human person. That's the message of ultimate dignity," said guest David Bobb, of the Bill of Rights Institute to 𝙏𝙝𝙞𝙨 𝘾𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙩𝙞𝙩𝙪𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣 host Matthew Brogdon. Learn more about striking a healthy balance between politics and human dignity in civic education in Season 3, Episode 19. Listen, http://bit.ly/4dLYuKY, or watch,  https://bit.ly/3BTJ5e7.

Big congratulations to Megan Thompson, Minnesota civics teacher, AP Gov instructor, and winner of the Civic Gold Star Award in the Civic Star Challenge 🏆 When her students needed to make sense of civil unrest happening in their own city, Megan turned to primary sources, founding principles, and the kind of teaching that lets students find their own answers. BRI and iCivics are proud to also recognize 20 Silver Star and 50 Bronze Star winners from classrooms across all 50 states. The Declaration of Independence is alive in America's classrooms this year, and teachers like Megan are why. There's a difference between a government you disagree with and a government that eliminates your voice entirely. The colonists understood that distinction clearly by the 1770s. When colonial assemblies pushed back against British taxation, King George III didn't negotiate, he dissolved them. Massachusetts first, then North Carolina and Virginia, then any colony that moved toward organizing a Continental Congress. Grievance 5 of the Declaration of Independence marks a turning point: the moment colonists stopped seeing British policy as overreach and started seeing it as tyranny. The shutdown of representative government wasn't a political disagreement anymore. It was a crisis of sovereignty. Episode 5 of our 27-part series is live. If the danger was going to come to our political institutions it would come from within. It would come from the alienation of people from their institutions and laws,  said host Matthew Brogdon to guest David Bobb of the Bill of Rights Institute on Season 3, Epsiode 19 of 𝙏𝙝𝙞𝙨 𝘾𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙩𝙞𝙩𝙪𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣. What does it take to undermine self-government without technically breaking the law? In Grievance 4, the colonists had an answer: move the meeting. Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG, Utah Valley University) returns to break down how royal governors used remote relocation orders to pressure colonial legislatures during moments of peak political tension, including Governor Gage forcing the Massachusetts Assembly out of Boston and into Salem in the lead-up to the Revolution. The tactic wasn't constant. But its occasional use was enough to demonstrate that colonial governance could be controlled from across the ocean. And for the colonists, the principle mattered as much as the pattern.
